steps.title: 32 patterns per word, propagated bitwise
steps.narrative: Resub, fraig, and dch all need to ask "do these two nodes compute the same function?" quickly. ABC's answer: simulate every node on the same set of input patterns packed 32-per-word, then hash on the resulting bitvector. Two nodes with different signatures are guaranteed different; two with the same signature are equivalence candidates (a real proof needs SAT).

entries.Allocation: fixed pool, one slot per AIG object

steps.title: Exhaustive 4-input enumeration with one word
steps.narrative: If the cut has 4 inputs, 32 patterns is enough to evaluate every input combination twice. ABC seeds the four input columns with these constants — convince yourself that bit i has (x0=bit0, x1=bit1, x2=bit2, x3=bit3) of the integer i mod 16 (and the upper 16 patterns repeat).

steps.title: Propagate bitwise through ANDs
steps.narrative: For each AIG AND, simulate via sim(z) = sim(p0) AND sim(p1), applying complements where edges are negated. This is a single 32-bit AND per node — extremely cheap. After one pass, every node carries a 32-bit signature.

panes.title: sim(f) = 0xFA88_FA88 — the truth table of x0·x1 + x0·x3 + x2·x3

steps.title: Two nodes with equal sim(): equivalence candidates
steps.narrative:
Resub looks for divisors whose sim signature matches the target node's. Hash the divisors' signatures into a bucket; when a target arrives, hash and look in the bucket. Hits are candidate equivalences; either:
  • they really are equivalent (then ABC may merge or substitute), or
  • they happen to agree on these 32 patterns but disagree on some unrepresented pattern (a spurious hit). Resub falls back to local truth-table verification; fraig falls back to SAT.

panes.body: fraig's dynamic patterns. When SAT returns SAT (i.e., it disproves equivalence), the counterexample is appended to the simulation vectors of all nodes (fraigSim.c). The next round of bucket-hashing benefits from a discriminating pattern, so spurious hits are rare in practice.

steps.title: Bigger cuts: more words
steps.narrative: For a k-input cut, 2^k input patterns are needed; that's nWords = max(1, 2^k / 32). resub's default nLeavesMax=8 → 256 patterns → 8 words. dch uses 8 words by default for richer pattern coverage even on small cuts. Operations stay bitwise: for (w=0; w<nWords; w++) sim_z[w] = sim_p0[w] & sim_p1[w];
